research.title: Migrating a Next.js Blog to Astro: When It's Worth It (and When It's Not)
research.whyTrending: Content sites are leaving Next.js for Astro (90% less JS, 2-3x faster loads); migration is typically 1-3 days with a compatible MDX pipeline. Pairs directly with the blog's heavy Next.js catalog and has strong search intent.

seo.suggestedTitle: Migrating a Next.js Blog to Astro: Worth It or Not?

gaps: Most are cheerleading migrations; few give an honest WHEN-NOT-TO decision up front
The React-components-as-islands reuse path underexplained (you dont rewrite everything)
Redirects/SEO preservation treated as afterthought when its the highest-risk step
getStaticProps -> content collections mapping shown shallowly
No realistic time/effort framing (1-3 days for a blog)
strengths: Real migration case studies with JS-cut numbers

officialSources.note: official migration guide: routing, MDX, components, data fetching

expertInsights: Migrate if the site is mostly static content with limited interactivity - gain speed, cut hosting; keep Next.js for heavy interactive apps/dashboards
Both use file-based routing, are component-based, and Astros syntax resembles JSX - migration is 1-3 days for a typical blog
MDX: bring files via @astrojs/mdx; adjust frontmatter (add layout or move to content collections)
Two content approaches: files in src/pages/ (file routing) OR content collections + dynamic getStaticPaths
React components reusable as islands: wrap <Component client:load /> to keep interactivity, or convert to .astro if static
getStaticProps/getServerSideProps -> Astro frontmatter fetch / import.meta.glob / fetch()
Remove Next-specific hooks (useRouter etc.), update import paths
Real result: ~45% less JS in one migration; Lighthouse SEO stayed 100; Astro edges Core Web Vitals
Highest-risk step: URL redirects + post-launch Search Console checks - dont change slugs without redirects
commonProblems: Rewriting every React component instead of reusing as islands
Changing URLs without redirects -> lost SEO
Trying to migrate a genuinely app-like site
statistics: ~45% JS reduction in a real migration
Astro 9KB vs Next.js 463KB JS on a comparable page (tested)

strategy.angle: Lead with the honest go/no-go decision (migrate content sites, keep apps) so readers dont migrate the wrong thing, then give a realistic 1-3 day playbook that emphasizes the two things that actually matter - reusing React components as islands (you dont rewrite everything) and preserving URLs/SEO with redirects (the highest-risk step)
strategy.title: Migrating a Next.js Blog to Astro: When It is Worth It (and When It Is Not)
strategy.excerpt: Content sites are leaving Next.js for Astro and cutting ~45% of their JavaScript. But migrate the wrong project and youll fight the framework. Heres the honest go/no-go, a realistic 1-3 day playbook, and the SEO-preserving redirect step everyone underestimates.
hooks: Contrarian: the first migration decision isnt how, its whether - and app-like sites should stay on Next.js
Number: one real migration cut 45% of JavaScript and kept a perfect Lighthouse SEO score
Gotcha: the risky part isnt the code, its changing URLs without redirects and losing your rankings
outline: Hook: the migration that shouldnt have happened
First: is your site a content site or an app?
What actually transfers (its more than you think)
The high-risk step: URLs, redirects, and SEO
